claim: Generative AI remains in the trough of disillusionment in the Gartner hype cycle as of 2025, having moved there much faster than other technologies like student information systems which took 8 years to reach the same stage.
actionable: Recognize that current AI implementations are still early-stage; expect disillusionment about hallucinations and bias before reaching enlightenment phase.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Only 11% of organizations surveyed reported clear return on investment from AI, while 74% either lost money or broke even on AI investments as of 2026.
actionable: When proposing AI investments, budget conservatively and plan for 2-3x higher total cost of ownership than initial estimates due to hidden costs in tokens, integration, and maintenance.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: For every one AI tool purchased, Gartner predicts approximately 10 hidden costs emerge including token utilization overages, cloud compute scaling, and legacy system integration.
actionable: Account for hidden costs like token consumption overage and system integration when forecasting AI spending; anticipate costs to increase 3-4x beyond day-one estimates.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Higher education institutions report very low tangible ROI from AI deployments—only 1% of higher ed institutions that deployed AI found it delivered tangible return on investment as of 2026.
actionable: Approach large-scale AI rollouts cautiously; pilot small use cases first and measure quantifiable outcomes before expanding.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: AI is not a magic band-aid and cannot replace bad data management, disconnected campus systems, or fundamental infrastructure problems; agentic AI cannot replace student information systems.
actionable: Before implementing AI solutions, audit your underlying data quality and system integration; fix infrastructure gaps first.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: There is a significant human readiness gap alongside AI readiness; most institutions have ~50% AI infrastructure readiness but only ~25% human readiness (literacy, governance, role redesign).
actionable: Invest equally in human literacy programs, governance frameworks, and role redesign as you do in AI infrastructure; neglecting human readiness will stall adoption.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Institutional adoption of AI tools lags behind industry pace; waiting for formal approvals can cause institutions to lose competitive edge on AI initiatives.
actionable: Establish fast-track approval processes for low-risk AI pilots to enable faculty experimentation without lengthy institutional review cycles.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Writing instructors report widespread use of ChatGPT by students to generate complete assignments; in-class writing with immediate grading is an effective counter-measure.
actionable: For writing-heavy courses, shift assessment to in-class timed writing on secure platforms (e.g., Blackboard essay mode) with instructor grading rather than take-home homework.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Using AI detection tools to catch AI-generated student work is unreliable and produces false positives that can harm innocent students.
actionable: Do not rely on AI detection tools for academic integrity decisions; instead use task redesign, transparency policies, and oral defense/in-class assessment.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Instructional AI (grading, plagiarism detection) generates the most legal liability and lawsuits in K-12 and higher ed due to lack of transparency and false plagiarism accusations.
actionable: If using AI in grading or plagiarism detection, require explicit informed consent from students, full transparency about tools used, and multiple human review gates.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Students benefit from iterative AI-assisted workflows where they use AI to enhance initial work (e.g., image generation from hand drawings) and document the process as part of their learning.
actionable: Design assignments that require students to show their iterative prompting process, refinements, and reflections on AI outputs to promote active learning over copy-paste.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Students struggle to evaluate the quality, accuracy, and bias in AI-generated outputs; they often accept AI answers without critical verification.
actionable: Explicitly teach students to fact-check AI outputs, verify sources, check reference accuracy, and develop healthy skepticism toward AI-generated information.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Structured AI use (e.g., Boodlebox integration with oversight) enables transparency; institutions using this approach see 20% faculty adoption with 100% expansion potential.
actionable: Implement AI partnership platforms with student/faculty oversight and prompt visibility; track student reasoning through AI tool prompts as part of assignment submission.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Faculty using generative AI primarily treat it as an instructor aid (test generation, draft ideas) rather than a teaching/learning tool; this underutilizes AI's pedagogical potential.
actionable: Redesign course learning objectives and assessments to leverage AI as an active learning tool (student practice, feedback, reasoning verification) not just faculty convenience.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Oral defenses and peer presentation of work are effective counter-measures to AI-enabled plagiarism; students must defend their answers and reasoning in real time.
actionable: Incorporate oral exams, presentations, or vivas where students defend their work in real-time conversation; this filters for understanding over copy-paste.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: In research, different LLMs produce significantly different results on identical datasets with same prompts; researchers must validate with multiple models and compare outputs before trusting results.
actionable: When using LLMs for data analysis or research, run the same analysis across 2-3 different models and compare results; treat LLM outputs as tentative until human validation.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: A deductive coding pipeline (LLM analyzed 3,200 teacher responses) achieved 98.3% accuracy when humans iterated in the loop, demonstrating value of human-AI collaboration in research.
actionable: For text analysis research, use LLMs in iterative human-in-the-loop pipelines with multiple model validation; this maintains data quality and provides grad student training.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Universities should use foundational AI literacy as part of general education for all freshmen, teaching hallucinations, bias, basic prompt mechanics, and decision-making with AI outputs.
actionable: Create a required gen-ed AI 101 course covering: what AI can/cannot do, hallucinations/bias, basic prompting, output verification, and ethical use.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: AI-ready graduates need experiential learning: practice in sandbox environments, group projects with AI tools, failure-based learning (correcting AI mistakes), and cross-disciplinary AI use.
actionable: Require all seniors/graduates to complete: simulated AI environment practice, group projects validating AI outputs, and at least one course using AI tools in their major.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Transparency is the key to responsible faculty AI use; as long as faculty clearly communicate what tools they use and why, different standards for faculty vs. student AI use are acceptable.
actionable: Document and communicate your AI tool usage to students; explain why different rules apply (goals differ); build student trust through openness.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: AI tools can unlock creative potential and self-expression for students with learning disabilities or neurodivergence (e.g., improving self-esteem through iterative art generation).
actionable: Consider AI tools as accessibility aids; they can enable students with dexterity or cognitive challenges to express themselves and build confidence in creative domains.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Institutional policy and governance lag 20+ years behind industry AI adoption; rapid pilot approval processes are needed to keep institutions competitive.
actionable: Create a fast-track governance pathway for low-risk AI pilots (e.g., faculty research, sandbox environments) separate from institution-wide rollouts.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel

claim: Distributed AI models built in-house by institutions (e.g., University of Sydney's platform) can be shared across multiple universities at scale; 45,000+ users across 90 institutions achieved ethically-trained, privacy-respecting tutoring.
actionable: For large-scale AI needs, consider partnerships with institutions building shared, ethically-trained models rather than purchasing multiple vendor licenses.
source_title: AI Day Keynote Speaker & Faculty Panel
